case_name: Mercury removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for mercury-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws raw water from a river-influenced reservoir affected by upstream legacy industrial contamination. The primary concern is total mercury at levels above typical drinking water goals, while the utility prefers established treatment approaches that can be operated by conventional plant staff. Existing plant capability supports coagulation, clarification, filtration, and disinfection, with only moderate tolerance for added complexity or energy use.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water mercury requirements and provide reliable turbidity and pathogen control for municipal distribution.
secondary_goals: use treatment steps compatible with conventional surface water plants

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional sludge handling is available but hazardous residual generation should be minimized
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with variable raw water turbidity is required

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize colloids and promote association of mercury with metal hydroxide flocs and natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.flocculation: grow settleable flocs containing particulate and adsorbed mercury
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk mercury-bearing floc and suspended solids
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: capture remaining particulate mercury and fine floc carryover
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon_polishing: reduce residual dissolved and organic-associated mercury through adsorption and organics control
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological safety for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: the raw water contains both particulate-bound and dissolved mercury, so a combined solids-removal and adsorption approach is well matched
surface water treatment with coagulation, clarification, filtration, and disinfection is conventional and proven for municipal plants
moderate DOC indicates that some mercury is likely associated with natural organic matter, making activated carbon polishing beneficial
this train aligns with the utility's existing conventional treatment capability and moderate constraints
potential_risks: mercury strongly complexed with dissolved organic matter may not be fully removed by coagulation alone
sludge and spent carbon containing mercury require controlled handling and disposal
performance depends on optimized coagulant dose and pH control during raw water quality changes

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: adsorb dissolved and organic-associated mercury before solids separation
core_unit_functions.coagulation: incorporate PAC and mercury-bearing colloids into floc
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove PAC and floc solids containing adsorbed mercury
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: polish remaining particulates and mercury-bearing fines

applicability_rationale: PAC addition can strengthen removal where dissolved or DOC-associated mercury is more important
the train still uses familiar conventional surface water operations
this is a practical upgrade path for plants that already use coagulation and clarification
it remains lower energy and more conventional than membrane-based alternatives
potential_risks: PAC dose requirements may vary significantly with DOC and seasonal water quality
increased solids production can complicate residuals handling
underfeeding PAC may leave dissolved mercury insufficiently treated

core_unit_functions.coagulation: promote attachment of particulate and colloidal mercury to floc
core_unit_functions.flocculation: increase floc size for downstream separation
core_unit_functions.dissolved_air_flotation_or_sedimentation: separate mercury-bearing floc from the treated stream
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: remove remaining suspended matter and associated mercury

applicability_rationale: a fully conventional solids-removal train is appropriate where most mercury is particulate-bound
the measured particulate-bound fraction is significant, supporting strong performance from clarification plus filtration
this approach is familiar, robust, and relatively low energy for municipal surface water treatment
it may be preferred if carbon systems are not yet installed
potential_risks: dissolved mercury can remain after effective particle removal
high DOC episodes may reduce metals removal by maintaining dissolved complexes
seasonal shifts in mercury partitioning can reduce consistency if treatment is not optimized

core_unit_functions.coagulation: convert fine colloidal and mercury-associated matter into more removable particles
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration_or_microfiltration: physically retain floc, suspended solids, and particulate-bound mercury
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon_post_treatment: polish residual dissolved and organic-associated mercury

applicability_rationale: membrane clarification can provide strong particulate control and stable turbidity performance
post-carbon improves control of the dissolved mercury fraction
this can be technically effective for plants seeking compact and high-barrier treatment
potential_risks: higher operational complexity and membrane maintenance burden than conventional clarification
membranes alone do not adequately address dissolved mercury without adsorption support
fouling risk increases with DOC and variable surface water quality

core_unit_functions.high_pressure_membrane_filtration: provide broad dissolved contaminant rejection including some mercury species
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after aggressive treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes can reduce dissolved contaminants and provide a strong treatment barrier
this option may be considered only when simultaneous removal of multiple dissolved contaminants is also needed
potential_risks: concentrate management creates a significant mercury-containing waste stream
energy demand and O&M complexity are excessive for an easy, single-contaminant case with conventional constraints
pretreatment needs can be substantial for variable surface water

preferred_rank_pattern: conventional coagulation_clarification_filtration based trains should rank at or near the top for mercury in surface water
options that add activated carbon for dissolved or organic-associated mercury should rank above high-energy membrane-only solutions
high-pressure membrane processes should not rank first under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints

must_reflect: preference for proven municipal surface water treatment processes
moderate cost and energy limits that disfavor advanced high-pressure membrane systems
compatibility with conventional plant staffing and operations
need to manage mercury-bearing residuals without creating unnecessary waste complexity

must_contain: the importance of distinguishing particulate-bound versus dissolved mercury
why coagulation clarification and filtration are suitable for the particulate-associated fraction
why activated carbon can improve control of dissolved or DOC-associated mercury
why membrane options rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: mercury complexation with natural organic matter
hazardous handling considerations for sludge or spent adsorbent
seasonal raw water variability and treatment optimization needs
concentrate management concerns for membrane alternatives
